content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : Robert Summers, Chief
Political Reform Audit Division
Franchise Tax Board

Date : Sept. 14, 1978

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
Barbara Campbell/[illegible]

Subject: Campaign Reporting Problem

This is in response to your memorandum dated December 2, 1977,
concerning the proper reporting of a procedure used by a large
central committee. I apologize for the inordinate delay in our
response.

Your memorandum outlined the following situation: A central
committee organized a controlled committee ("Special Election
Account") into which small contributions to the central committee
were deposited. Additionally some of the central committee's
funds were transferred into the Special Election Account. Monetary
and in-kind contribution expenditures were made from this Special
Election Account on behalf of special election candidates. None of
the contributions transferred from the central committee were
earmarked for special election activity.

The Special Election Account reported the detail of the contributions deposited by the central committee as if the contributions
were received directly by the Special Election Account. You asked
for our comment on that procedure.

In the absence of any facts indicating the contributions to
the central committee were earmarked by the contributor for special
election activity,1/ we conclude that the central committee should
have reported the receipt of all contributions. The funds that
the central committee decided to turn over to the Special Election
Account should be reported by the Special Election Account as a
transfer from the central committee.

I hope this answers your concern. If you have any other
questions, please contact me.

1/ If the contributions to the central committee were in fact
earmarked by the contributor for the Special Election Account, then
we believe the proper reporting method would have been for the
Special Election Account to report the receipt of the contribution
from each contributor with the central committee also listed as
the intermediary.

BC:ph

Robert C. Summers
Franchise Tax Board

Sept. 3, 1978

Lee C. Rosenthal

Your memo of August 23, 1978

Thank you for your reminder. Your December 2, 1977
memo concerning the use of special accounts got lost in
the shuffle. We will discuss the issue and provide you
with a reply within the next two weeks.

State of California

Memorandum

Franchise Tax Board

To     :    Michael Bennett, Executive Director
            Fair Political Practices Commission
            1100 K Street
            Sacramento, CA 95814

Date   :    December 2, 1977

File No.:   78017

From   :    Robert C. Summers

Subject :   Campaign Reporting Problem

The following described procedure was discovered during the audit of a large
central committee and we would like your advice regarding the propriety of
the procedure and whether or not the transactions were properly reported.

In an effort to eliminate the need to file detailed campaign statements due
to special election activity, the central committee organized a controlled
committee titled Special Election Account. Small contributions, sent to the
central committee, were deposited into the Special Election Account and, in
addition, some funds were transferred from the central committee's account.
It is our understanding that the contributions deposited were not earmarked
for this special activity. Monetary and in-kind contribution expenditures
were made, in behalf of the special election candidates, from the Special
Election Account.

The Special Election Account committee reported the detail of the contributions
received by the central committee as though the contributions were received
directly. The transfer of funds and the expenditures were properly reported.

It would seem to me that the controlled committee should disclose that the
central committee is the sole source of its funds, or at least they should
be shown as an intermediary.

Another problem illustrated by this case is the ease with which some committees
deposit contributions in a variety of associated accounts which are not
specifically earmarked by the contributor. We have found many instances,
through confirmation letters, where the contributor was under the impression
that the gift was to a completely different entity.

We would appreciate your advice as to the proper reporting procedure in the
illustrated case.

Robert C. Summers

Chief, Political Reform Audit Division

cc :   D. Converse
       D. Dick
       J. Turrini
       N. Fuller

sections.question: The Franchise Tax Board requests FPPC advice regarding: (1) the propriety of a central committee organizing a controlled committee titled 'Special Election Account' to eliminate filing detailed campaign statements, with contributions and transferred funds deposited into and expenditures made from this account, and whether the transactions were properly reported; (2) whether the controlled committee should disclose that the central committee is the sole source of its funds or show the central committee as an intermediary; and (3) the proper reporting procedure when contributions are deposited into associated accounts not specifically earmarked by the contributor, where contributors may be unaware of which entity received their gift.
sections.question_synthetic: The Franchise Tax Board seeks FPPC guidance on whether a central committee's creation of a controlled 'Special Election Account' committee to avoid detailed filing requirements complies with campaign finance law, and whether such controlled committees must disclose their funding sources and the central committee's intermediary role.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request from Franchise Tax Board to FPPC, not an FPPC advice letter response. The document includes both the original December 2, 1977 memo from Robert C. Summers and an acknowledgment dated March 8, 1978 from Lee C. Rosenthal. No formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION sections present; question reconstructed from memo content. The response was promised but not included in this document.

embedding.summary: This is an incoming request from the California Franchise Tax Board to the FPPC seeking legal advice regarding campaign finance reporting issues discovered during an audit, specifically concerning a central committee's use of a controlled 'Special Election Account' to circumvent filing requirements. The FPPC acknowledged receipt and promised a response within two weeks.
